Figure 2: Cross-category dashboard: one representative dataset per research area, every compatible architecture trained across the same ten seeds.

Figure 1: The GRAPHNETZ evaluation protocol. Top: the processing pipeline Catalog→Encoders→Training→Statistical layer R; arrows carry the data axes – a (task t, data) pair, a model m, and the seed-paired metric tensor X[t,m, s]. The dashed arc on Training marks the multi-seed inner loop that builds the seed axis of X . Bottom: the three artifacts the framework emits by default – per-cell mean with Student’s t confidence interval, a Holm-corrected pairwise p-value matrix, and a Demšar critical-difference diagram whose horizontal bar joins models within the Nemenyi threshold CD.

Figure 4: Anatomy of a cross-category Demšar critical-difference (CD) diagram. Top: the CDα reference scale, fixed by k and N through CDα = qα √ k(k + 1)/(6N), calibrates which mean-rank gaps are large enough to be significant. Middle: each model is plotted at its mean rank across the N tasks; lower is better. Bottom: clique bars connect contiguous runs of models whose total mean-rank span is below CDα, they are not significantly different from one another at level α. Models that share no bar (here A and C) are declared significantly different. The diagram is the standard scalable view when the benchmark spans many tasks; in this paper it appears as Figure 3 with k = 4 encoders and N = 10 tasks, where CD0.05 = 1.48 exceeds the largest observed mean-rank gap and all four architectures fall in a single Nemenyi clique.

Figure 3: Cross-category Demšar critical-difference (CD) diagram at α = 0.05 across N = 10 tasks. The diagram is restricted to the four node-level encoders (GCN, GAT, GraphSAGE, Graph Transformer) that are present in every category.
Table 4: Dataset loaders per research category entries. Note: NC node classification, GC graph classification, GR graph regression, LP link prediction.
